# Budgeting

<br>

Let's get one thing straight upfront: **most people hate budgeting because they're doing it wrong.** Traditional budgets fail because they treat humans like robots—tracking every penny, restricting every joy, and demanding perfection. The frameworks below, drawn from Sethi, Housel, Bogle, and Felix, are designed for how humans actually behave.

> "A budget tells your money where to go instead of wondering where it went." — A principle Ramit Sethi often echoes

## The Budget Mindset Shift

Before tactics, the mindset: a budget isn't about restriction—it's about **conscious allocation**. Morgan Housel's insight applies powerfully here: **room for error is the most underrated financial concept.** A good budget builds in flexibility, not rigidity.

```{=html}
<style>

.codeStyle span:not(.nodeLabel) {
  font-family: monospace;
  font-size: 1.5em;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: #9753b8 !important;
  background-color: #f6f6f6;
  padding: 0.2em;
}
</style>
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Budget Mindset'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    OldBudget[Old Budget Mindset] --> Restrict[Restrict Everything]
    Restrict --> Fail[Fails Within Weeks]
    
    NewBudget[Conscious Spending Plan] --> Prioritize[Prioritize What Matters]
    Prioritize --> Sustain[Sustains for Years]
```

## Fixed vs. Variable Expenses: Know the Difference

The first skill in budgeting is correctly categorizing your money. Most people lump everything together and get overwhelmed.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Fixed vs. Variable Expenses'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart LR
    Expenses[Monthly Expenses] --> Fixed[FIXED EXPENSES<br/>Predictable & Recurring]
    Expenses --> Variable[VARIABLE EXPENSES<br/>Fluctuate Month to Month]
    Expenses --> Periodic[PERIODIC EXPENSES<br/>Annual or Irregular]
    
    Fixed --> Rent[Rent/Mortgage]
    Fixed --> Insurance[Insurance Premiums]
    Fixed --> Subs[Subscriptions]
    Fixed --> Loans[Loan Payments]
    
    Variable --> Groceries[Groceries]
    Variable --> Utilities[Utilities]
    Variable --> Gas[Gas/Transport]
    Variable --> Dining[Dining Out]
    
    Periodic --> Taxes[Property Taxes]
    Periodic --> CarMaint[Car Maintenance]
    Periodic --> Gifts[Gifts/Holidays]
    Periodic --> Medical[Medical Costs]
```

### Why This Matters

| Category | Strategy |
|----------|----------|
| **Fixed Expenses** | Lock these in and forget them. The goal is to **minimize them once**, then automate. Biggest leverage point: housing. |
| **Variable Expenses** | Where most "budgeting" effort fails. Use **categories with limits**, not line-by-line tracking. |
| **Periodic Expenses** | The silent budget-killer. Use a **sinking fund**—set aside 1/12 of the annual cost each month. |

### The Sinking Fund: Your Secret Weapon

If your car insurance is $1,200/year, don't get blindsided every six months. Save $100/month into a "Car Insurance" sub-account. When the bill arrives, the money is already there. Repeat for every periodic expense:

- Christmas/gifts: $600/year → $50/month
- Car maintenance: $1,200/year → $100/month
- Annual vacation: $2,400/year → $200/month

This single technique eliminates 80% of "budget emergencies."

## Zero-Based vs. Percentage Budgets

Two dominant frameworks exist. Both work—pick the one that matches your personality.

### Zero-Based Budgeting

Every dollar gets a job. Income minus all allocations equals zero. Popularized by tools like YNAB (You Need A Budget).

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Zero-Based Budgeting'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart LR
    Income[$5,000 Income] --> Rent2[$1,500 Rent]
    Income --> Food[$500 Groceries]
    Income --> Transport[$300 Transport]
    Income --> Invest[$1,000 Investments]
    Income --> Save[$500 Savings]
    Income --> Fun[$700 Fun Money]
    Income --> Misc[$500 Other]
    Misc --> Zero[Total = $5,000<br/>Remaining = $0]
```

**Best for:** Detail-oriented people, those rebuilding from debt, irregular income earners, anyone needing tight control.

**Pros:** Maximum awareness, every dollar intentional, exposes leaks immediately.

**Cons:** High maintenance, can feel restrictive, requires re-budgeting monthly.

### Percentage-Based Budgeting

Allocate by percentages, not dollars. This is the foundation of Sethi's Conscious Spending Plan and similar frameworks (50/30/20, etc.).

| Bucket | Conscious Spending Plan (Sethi) | 50/30/20 Rule |
|--------|--------------------------------|---------------|
| Fixed Costs | 50–60% | 50% Needs |
| Investments | 10%+ | 20% Savings/Debt |
| Savings Goals | 5–10% | (combined above) |
| Guilt-Free Spending | 20–35% | 30% Wants |

**Best for:** Big-picture thinkers, stable income earners, those who hate detailed tracking.

**Pros:** Simple, sustainable, scales with income, low maintenance.

**Cons:** Less granular, can mask category overspending, requires honesty about category boundaries.

### Which Should You Choose?

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Which Budget To Choose?'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    Choose[Which Budget Style?] --> Question1{In Debt or<br/>Just Starting?}
    Question1 -->|Yes| ZeroBased[Zero-Based<br/>for 6-12 months]
    Question1 -->|No| Question2{Love Spreadsheets<br/>& Details?}
    Question2 -->|Yes| Hybrid[Hybrid Approach]
    Question2 -->|No| Percentage[Percentage-Based<br/>Conscious Spending]
    ZeroBased --> Eventually[Graduate to Percentage<br/>Once Stable]
```

**My honest recommendation:** Start with zero-based for 2-3 months to truly understand your spending. Then transition to a percentage-based system with automation. You get the awareness without the long-term burden.

## Common Budgeting Tools and Spreadsheets

### Apps

| Tool | Best For | Cost |
|------|----------|------|
| **YNAB (You Need A Budget)** | Zero-based devotees, debt payoff | ~$15/month |
| **Monarch Money** | Couples, net worth tracking | ~$15/month |
| **Empower (formerly Personal Capital)** | Investment + budget overview | Free |
| **Copilot** | iOS users, beautiful design | ~$13/month |
| **Rocket Money** | Subscription cancellation, simple tracking | Free/Premium |
| **PocketGuard** | Simple "what can I spend?" view | Free/Premium |

### The Spreadsheet Approach

Bogle would approve: **simplicity wins.** A well-built spreadsheet beats most apps because it forces you to engage with your numbers. Here's a basic structure:

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Spreadsheet'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart LR
    Spreadsheet[Budget Spreadsheet] --> Tab1[Tab 1: Income]
    Spreadsheet --> Tab2[Tab 2: Fixed Expenses]
    Spreadsheet --> Tab3[Tab 3: Variable Expenses]
    Spreadsheet --> Tab4[Tab 4: Sinking Funds]
    Spreadsheet --> Tab5[Tab 5: Net Worth Tracker]
    Spreadsheet --> Tab6[Tab 6: Summary Dashboard]
    
    Tab6 --> Metrics[Key Metrics:<br/>Savings Rate<br/>Fixed Cost %<br/>Monthly Cash Flow]
```

**Free options to get started:**

- Google Sheets templates (search "budget template")    
- Tiller Money (~$79/year—automates bank imports into Sheets)   
- The classic envelope spreadsheet from any personal finance blog   

### What Actually Matters in a Tool

Don't overthink this. The best budgeting tool is **the one you'll actually use.** 

Features that matter:

1. **Automatic transaction imports** (saves hours per month)
2. **Easy categorization** (you'll do this weekly)
3. **A clear summary view** (your dashboard)
4. **Goal tracking** (motivation matters)

Features that don't matter as much: fancy graphs, AI insights, social features. Substance over style.

## The Math You Need to Master

Here's the secret most personal finance content avoids: **budgeting requires only middle-school math.** But you need to be *fluent* in a few specific calculations.

### 1. Percentages (Your Most-Used Skill)

You need to instantly answer: "What percentage of my income is X?"

**Formula:** (Expense ÷ Income) × 100

Example: Rent is $1,500, income is $5,000. → 1,500 ÷ 5,000 = 0.30 → **30% of income on rent.**

Practice until you can do this in your head for the big categories.

### 2. Savings Rate (Your Most Important Metric)

Felix and Bogle both stress this above all else.

**Formula:** (Savings + Investments) ÷ Gross Income

Example: You save $500 and invest $700 from a $5,000 gross income. → 1,200 ÷ 5,000 = **24% savings rate.**

Benchmarks to know:

- **<10%:** Danger zone—build aggressively    
- **10-20%:** Average—you'll retire eventually    
- **20-30%:** Strong—you're building real wealth    
- **30%+:** Path to early financial independence    

### 3. Annualizing and Monthly-izing

The skill of converting between time periods. Budgets fail because people don't see annual costs in monthly terms (or vice versa).

**Annual → Monthly:** Divide by 12

**Monthly → Annual:** Multiply by 12

**Weekly → Monthly:** Multiply by 4.33 (not 4)

**Daily habit → Annual:** Multiply by 365

Example: A $5 daily coffee → $5 × 365 = **$1,825/year.** Suddenly it's a real number you can evaluate.

### 4. Compound Interest (The Wealth Equation)

You don't need to calculate this by hand, but you need to *understand* it.

**Formula:** FV = PV × (1 + r)^n

Where: FV = future value, PV = present value, r = annual return, n = years

**The shortcut you should memorize—the Rule of 72:**

> Years to double your money = 72 ÷ annual return %

At 7% return, money doubles every ~10 years. At 10%, every ~7 years. This single insight reframes every spending decision.

### 5. Opportunity Cost Math

This is Felix's signature framework. **Every dollar spent isn't just that dollar—it's what that dollar could have become.**

**Formula:** Cost × (1 + r)^n = Opportunity cost in future dollars

Example: A $40,000 new car vs. a $15,000 used car. The $25,000 difference, invested at 7% real return over 30 years:

$25,000 × (1.07)^30 = **~$190,000**

That's not a car payment difference—that's a retirement chapter.

### 6. Break-Even and Payback Calculations

Useful for evaluating subscriptions, refinances, and big purchases.

**Formula:** Upfront cost ÷ monthly savings = months to break even

Example: A $500 annual membership saves you $60/month → 500 ÷ 60 = **8.3 months to break even.** Worth it if you'll use it longer than that.

## Putting It All Together: A Budget That Actually Works

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: 'Put It All Together'
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    Start[Build Your Budget] --> Step1[1. Track Spending<br/>for 30 Days]
    Step1 --> Step2[2. Categorize Fixed vs.<br/>Variable vs. Periodic]
    Step2 --> Step3[3. Calculate Current<br/>Savings Rate]
    Step3 --> Step4[4. Choose Framework<br/>Zero-Based or Percentage]
    Step4 --> Step5[5. Set Up Sinking Funds<br/>for Periodic Expenses]
    Step5 --> Step6[6. Automate Everything<br/>Investments First]
    Step6 --> Step7[7. Review Weekly,<br/>Adjust Monthly]
    Step7 --> Step8[8. Recalibrate Quarterly]
```

## The Bottom Line

A budget is just a **plan for your money in advance.** The best one:

1. **Matches your personality.** Don't fight your nature—work with it.
2. **Automates the boring stuff.** Investments, bills, sinking funds—set and forget.
3. **Leaves room for joy.** Sethi's guilt-free spending isn't optional; it's what makes budgets sustainable.
4. **Tracks one number above all: your savings rate.** Everything else is detail.
5. **Builds in margin for error.** Housel's principle—reality is messier than spreadsheets.

The math isn't hard. The categories aren't complicated. The tools are abundant. What's actually required is **the decision to be intentional**—and the patience to let small, consistent allocations compound into a life of financial freedom.

As Bogle reminded us about investing, the same applies to budgeting: **don't do something, just stand there.** Set up the system, then let it work.
